description: Roulette activities for each Wheel of Life dimension. Each activity is specific enough to be actionable but loose enough to not feel like a chore. Designed for immediate, low-effort engagement.

healthBody.label: Health & Body
activities: Fill a big glass of water and drink the whole thing before you do anything else.
Stand up, go outside for exactly 5 minutes, and come back. No destination needed.
Do 10 slow neck rolls — 5 each direction. You've been sitting weird.
Eat one piece of fruit or something that grew in the ground. Right now.
Set a 7-minute timer and stretch whatever feels the tightest. Just floor stretches, no routine.
Walk to the furthest point of wherever you are and walk back. That counts.
Close your eyes and take 5 breaths where the exhale is twice as long as the inhale.
Make yourself a proper meal for the next mealtime — not cereal, not toast. Something real.
Do 10 squats next to wherever you're sitting. Get it over with.
Go to bed 30 minutes earlier tonight. Decide that now, put it in your head.

mentalWellbeing.label: Mental & Emotional Wellbeing
activities: Write 3 sentences about what's actually bothering you right now. Not what you think should bother you — what actually is.
Text your therapist or check when your next appointment is. If you don't have one booked, book one.
Name the emotion you're feeling right now. Not good or bad — the actual word. Sit with it for 60 seconds.
Think of one thing that happened today that wasn't terrible. Write it down somewhere.
Put on a song that matches exactly how you feel right now, not one to change the mood. Just match it.
Say out loud to yourself: 'I'm doing the best I can with what I've got right now.' Once is enough.
Write down one thing you're worried about and one concrete reason it might be okay.
Sit somewhere quiet for 4 minutes and do nothing. No phone. Just exist.
Think of someone who makes you feel calm just by existing. Send them a message.
Rate your stress from 1–10 and ask yourself: what would drop it by 2 points? Just notice the answer.

relationships.label: Relationships
activities: Send a voice message to a friend instead of a text. Just say hi and something real.
Message someone you've been meaning to catch up with and actually lock in a date before you close the chat.
Send a meme or a photo to someone you care about with zero context. Just thinking of you.
Call someone on the way to or from something. Even 5 minutes counts.
Reply to someone's story or post with more than an emoji — write something actual.
Think of the friend you've been worst at keeping in touch with lately and reach out first.
Send a message to a friend that's just checking in on them — make it about them, not you.
Make a plan that you'll actually keep — dinner, a walk, anything. Put it in the calendar.
Tell someone something specific you appreciate about them. Out loud or in a message.
Look at your camera roll and find a photo with someone you love. Send it to them.

family.label: Family
activities: Say good morning or goodnight to whoever is home. Actually stop and do it properly.
Ask mum or dad one question about their day and listen to the full answer.
Do one household thing that isn't yours to do without mentioning that you did it.
Sit in the same room as family for 20 minutes even if you're doing your own thing.
Make tea or a snack and bring one to someone else in the house without being asked.
Call or message a family member you haven't spoken to in a while. Doesn't have to be long.
Think of one thing a family member is going through right now and acknowledge it to them.
Look at an old family photo and let yourself feel something about it.
Do one errand or task that takes something off someone else's plate today.
Put your phone down for the next family meal and just be there.

workCareer.label: Work & Career
activities: Pick the one thing on your to-do list that would make tomorrow feel easier and do just that.
Spend 15 minutes on a project that matters to you — not urgent, just meaningful.
Write down one thing you're proud of that you've made or shipped in the last month.
Clear your inbox or task list of anything that's been sitting there longer than 2 weeks — done, delegated, or deleted.
Spend 10 minutes learning one small thing that's directly useful to your work right now.
Write the first paragraph of something you've been putting off. Just the first one.
Identify the one task you've been avoiding the most and do 10 minutes of it. Just 10.
Sketch out what 'done' looks like for your current biggest project. Not a plan — just the picture of done.
Close all tabs that aren't related to what you're supposed to be doing right now.
Make a note of one thing you want to get better at this year. Put it somewhere you'll see it.

creativeArt.label: Creative Life & Art
activities: Draw a 5-minute doodle of the last person you spoke to. No erasing. Just go.
Open a sketchbook and draw the first object you see from where you're sitting. Give it 10 minutes.
Pick a colour you love and fill a page with things that are that colour. No plan, just fill it.
Try to draw something from memory — a face, a place, an object. See what comes out.
Do a 1-minute gesture drawing of your own hand in whatever position it's resting.
Write the first line of something — a story, a letter, a poem. Just one line. See where it goes.
Find a reference image of something you find beautiful and try to capture the feeling of it, not the detail.
Open a creative programming project and spend 20 minutes on it with no goal except curiosity.
Draw something you ate today. Ugly is fine. Done is the point.
Pick a technique you've been meaning to try and just attempt it badly for 15 minutes.

restRecovery.label: Rest & Recovery
activities: Put something on your intentional watch list — not the algorithm, something you chose — and actually watch it.
Read manga or a book for 20 minutes with your phone in another room.
Put on an album you love from start to finish and don't do anything else while it plays.
Play Animal Crossing or a low-stakes game for 30 minutes without guilt.
Go for a walk with no podcast, no music, no destination. Just look at things.
Make a hot drink, sit somewhere comfortable, and drink it slowly without your phone.
Watch one episode of something comforting that you've seen before.
Do absolutely nothing for 10 minutes. Lie on the floor if you have to. Just stop.
Run a bath or a long shower with no rush. Use the nice stuff.
Pick one thing from your 'to watch / to read / to listen to' list and start it tonight.

nourishment.label: Nourishment
activities: Go somewhere alone for 30 minutes — a walk, a café, the car, anywhere. No one needs anything from you.
Put your phone on do not disturb and spend the next hour doing whatever you want with no one watching.
Play a game, watch something, or do something creative entirely for yourself with no productivity justification.
Eat a meal alone in peace — no phone, no TV, no one to talk to. Just eat.
Leave the house by yourself and go anywhere that isn't a chore. Window shopping, a walk, a drive.
Spend 20 minutes doing something completely unplanned just because you felt like it in the moment.
Book yourself a solo experience — a class, a cinema trip, a coffee alone at a place you like.
Put headphones in and listen to something only you care about for the length of a commute.
Say no to one thing today that you didn't actually want to do. Just once.
Spend an evening doing nothing that benefits anyone but yourself. Let that be enough.
